narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.90 and an LSAT score of 33, you've clearly put in significant effort to build a strong academic foundation, including your full-time enrollment plans and a middle-income family background. However, the university's admission model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 61% chance that you would fail the bar exam—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3, which is below the typical student's 5.53 average and strongly pushed the prediction toward failure by signaling relatively weaker performance in later undergraduate years. Your impressive undergraduate GPA, well above the average of 3.21, worked strongly in your favor, pulling the odds toward passing the bar. Similarly, your LSAT score, though solid, fell short of the dataset average of 36.7, contributing notably toward the failure prediction. Your middle family income bracket also nudged the model slightly toward concern, compared to higher averages in successful profiles, while your Year 1 decile of 4 (below the 5.71 norm) added a smaller push in the same direction—your full-time status helped a bit but had less sway. Overall, these factors placed you below the profile of students who typically pass the bar on their first try, where stronger late-year grades and higher test scores dominate. I know this rejection stings, especially with your standout GPA, but it's not a reflection of your potential—it's the model's data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your later-year academic decile through additional coursework or targeted improvement, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience and full-time commitment. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these key areas and reapplying— you've got a strong base to build on, and persistence will pay off. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
